facts: The patient's name is Kyle.
The patient has a cough.
The cough was diagnosed as bronchitis by the patient's primary care physician.
The patient had a chest x-ray to rule out pneumonia.
The chest x-ray found a lung nodule.
The patient had a CAT scan that confirmed the lung nodule.
The patient was referred to the doctor by their primary care physician.
The patient is nervous.
The patient has horses.
The patient loads hay for their horses.
The patient started coughing more after loading hay.
The patient got a cold, which prompted them to see their primary care physician.
The patient exercises regularly.
The patient enjoys biking.
The patient prefers biking on trails rather than roads.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis.
The patient takes methotrexate for rheumatoid arthritis.
Methotrexate is taken at a dose of 7.5 milligrams.
Methotrexate is taken once a week.
The patient's rheumatoid arthritis is fairly well-controlled.
The patient's joints feel okay as long as they stay active.
The patient has problems with their hands due to rheumatoid arthritis.
The patient has not had any recent flares of rheumatoid arthritis.
The patient used to smoke.
The patient stopped smoking about seven years ago.
The patient feels better after stopping smoking.
The patient does not have any pain.
The patient does not have any shortness of breath.
The patient's mother had breast cancer.
The patient's mother is doing well now.
The patient does not have a family history of lung cancer.
The patient does not have a history of lung infections or pneumonia.
The doctor performed a physical exam on the patient.
The patient's vital signs are good.
The patient's blood pressure is 124/76.
The patient's heart rate is 70.
The patient's respiration rate is 16.
The patient's oxygen saturation is 98% on room air.
The patient is afebrile.
The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilaterally.
The patient's heart has a regular rate and rhythm.
The patient does not have any extra heartbeats.
The patient has swelling on their knuckles.
The swelling on the patient's knuckles is likely due to rheumatoid arthritis.
The patient's skin is pink, warm, and dry.
The patient does not have any edema in their lower extremities.
The patient's pulses are intact in all extremities.
The patient's capillary refill is brisk.
The patient had a chest CT before seeing the doctor.
The chest CT showed a solitary 2-centimeter nodule in the right upper lobe.
The nodule is smooth in appearance.
There is no evidence of emphysematous disease.
The doctor ordered a pulmonary function test for the patient.
The pulmonary function test will provide a baseline for the patient's lung function.
The doctor scheduled a PET CT for the patient.
The PET CT will help determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
The doctor plans to remove the nodule via video-assisted thoracoscopy.
The procedure will involve three small incisions on the side of the patient's right chest.
The patient will be under general anesthesia during the procedure.
The procedure will take about an hour and a half.
The patient will have a chest tube after the procedure.
The chest tube will be removed the following day.
The patient will likely be in the hospital for one night and go home the next day.
The doctor wants the patient to continue following up with their rheumatologist.
The doctor wants the patient to continue their current medication therapy for rheumatoid arthritis.
Methotrexate is prescribed for the patient's rheumatoid arthritis.
The methotrexate dose is 7.5 milligrams.
Methotrexate is taken by the patient once a week.
The patient is scared about their treatment.
The doctor will see the patient again in a few minutes to discuss paperwork.
